metadata.description: Page heading 'State Machine' is rendered at the top with a Network icon
Configuration selector dropdown is present with placeholder 'Select configuration...' showing config name and render count for each option
A refresh button is present to reload configurations. Shows a spinning icon while loading.
Discovery tab is present with Search icon for discovering states from SDK apps
Graph Editor tab is present with GitBranch icon for visual state/transition editing
State View tab is present with Layers icon for browsing and editing states
Transitions tab is present with ArrowRightLeft icon for managing transition definitions and action sequences
Pathfinding tab is present with Route icon for finding optimal paths between states
Export tab is present with Download icon for downloading or pushing the state machine to the runner

Clicking a tab switches the visible content panel. The active tab has bg-background, shadow-sm, and text-foreground styling. Inactive tabs use text-muted-foreground. All tab content panels remain in the DOM (hidden via CSS class 'hidden') to preserve state when switching tabs.
The 'State Machine' heading uses text-lg (1.125rem/18px) font-semibold with text-text-primary color
The Network icon next to the heading uses the brand-primary color (text-brand-primary class)
The header bar uses px-6 (24px horizontal) and py-4 (16px vertical) padding with border-b border-border-primary

The active tab button has a white/light background (bg-background), subtle shadow (shadow-sm), and foreground text color (text-foreground), visually distinguishing it from inactive tabs that use text-muted-foreground
Tab icons use size-3.5 (14px) and tab text uses text-sm (14px) font-medium
All tab labels (Discovery, Graph Editor, State View, Transitions, Pathfinding, Export) are visible against the navigation bar background. Active tab text and inactive tab text must both be legible.

Muted instructional text (text-text-muted class) like 'Select a project to manage state machines' and 'Select a configuration to view the state graph' must be readable against their container backgrounds, not invisible.

Badge text in discovery results (state count, element count, render count, strategy) is visible against badge backgrounds. Both 'secondary' variant badges and 'outline' variant badges must have legible text.
Export panel text including config name, state count, and transition count (text-text-muted and text-text-primary) must be visible against the bg-surface-secondary card background

State name and confidence percentage text in the discovery results preview list must be visible. State names use text-text-primary and confidence uses text-text-muted — both must contrast against the bordered container background.
A message tells the user to select a project to manage state machines

value.textContent: Select a configuration to view the state graph

metadata.description: Graph Editor tab shows a message to select a configuration to view the state graph

value.textContent: Select a configuration to view states

metadata.description: State View tab shows a message to select a configuration to view states

value.textContent: Select a configuration to view transitions

metadata.description: Transitions tab shows a message to select a configuration to view transitions

value.textContent: Select a configuration to use pathfinding

metadata.description: Pathfinding tab shows a message to select a configuration
Export tab shows a message to select a configuration
A 'Connect to SDK App' label with Plug icon is shown at the top of the discovery panel
A 'Scan' button is present to scan for SDK-instrumented apps on the selected runner. Shows a spinning loader while scanning.
When no runners are detected and local runner is unavailable, a message tells the user: 'No runners detected. Start the qontinui-runner app to connect.'
When a runner is connected but no apps found yet, a message prompts: 'Click Scan to find apps with the UI Bridge SDK installed.'

When an SDK app is connected, a green banner shows 'Connected: {appName}' with a CheckCircle2 icon and the app URL in an outline badge. This banner uses bg-green-500/10 background with border-green-500/20 border.

When no backend WebSocket connections exist but the local runner (localhost:9876) is available, it is auto-selected in the runner dropdown as 'Local Runner (localhost:9876)' with a green MonitorSmartphone icon.
An 'Explore' tab with Compass icon is present for automated UI Bridge exploration
A 'Record' tab with Camera icon is present for manual snapshot recording
A 'Start Recording' button with a red Circle icon is present to begin manual snapshot capture
A 'Capture Now' button with Camera icon takes a single manual snapshot. Disabled when not recording and no snapshots exist.
Recording instructions text: 'Capture snapshots from the connected SDK app. Navigate the app manually while recording to capture different UI states.'

While recording, a red 'Stop Recording' button replaces 'Start Recording'. A pulsing red dot badge shows the snapshot count. After stopping, a 'Use N Snapshots for Discovery' button appears to proceed.

During and after recording, a badge shows '{count} snapshots'. While recording, the badge is destructive variant with a pulsing red dot. After recording stops, it's secondary variant. Next to the badge, text shows the element count of the last snapshot.
The Explore/Record collection tabs and their content are hidden when no SDK app is connected. They only become visible after a successful SDK app connection.
The Explore/Record collection tabs and their content are hidden when no SDK app is connected. They only become visible after a successful SDK app connection.
After collection, a 'Render Logs Collected' card shows with a Layers icon, badge showing '{count} render logs', and a badge showing the collection source ('via explore' or 'via record')

A 'Discover States' button with Sparkles icon triggers the state discovery algorithm. Shows a spinning loader while discovering. The button text reads 'Run state discovery on the collected render logs to identify UI states.'
A 'Start Over' button with RotateCcw icon allows the user to discard collected renders and restart the discovery process. Clears localStorage persisted state.
After discovery completes, a 'Configuration Name' labeled input field is shown with placeholder 'e.g., My App States'. The 'Save to Project' button is disabled until a name is entered.

A 'Save to Project' button with Save icon persists the discovered state machine configuration via POST /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/ui-bridge-discover. Disabled when no config name is entered or while saving.
After discovery completes, a 'Discovery Complete' card heading is shown with a green CheckCircle2 icon
Discovery results show a secondary badge with the number of states discovered (e.g., '3 states')
Discovery results show a secondary badge with the total number of unique elements found (e.g., '15 elements')
Discovery results show an outline badge with the render count used for discovery (e.g., '5 renders')
When the discovery result includes strategy_used, an outline badge shows the strategy name (e.g., 'signature strategy' or 'fingerprint strategy'). This indicates which algorithm was used.

A scrollable preview list (max-h-40) shows each discovered state with its name (text-text-primary) on the left and confidence percentage (text-text-muted) on the right, formatted as '{confidence * 100}% confidence'

In the discovery results preview, the 'elements' badge count should reflect the total number of unique elements found across all renders. After saving and viewing in the Graph Editor, the union of all element tiles across all state nodes should account for all discovered elements. If elements were dropped, the discovery algorithm lost data; if extra elements appear, elements were duplicated.

Every state in the discovery results preview shows a confidence score between 0% and 100%. Confidence reflects how consistently the state's element pattern appeared across the input renders. A confidence of 100% means the elements always co-occurred in every render where any of them appeared. Confidence below 50% may indicate weak grouping that should be reviewed.

The number of discovered states should be reasonable relative to the input renders. Given N renders with K distinct element configurations, the algorithm should produce approximately K states (not more than N, as each render belongs to exactly one state). If the algorithm produces 0 states from non-empty renders, or produces more states than renders, it indicates a fundamental algorithm error visible in the discovery results badges.

After saving a configuration via 'Save to Project', the discovery state is cleared (renders, results, config name all reset). The localStorage entry is removed. The user returns to the initial collection phase.
A 'Fit to view' button auto-fits the graph in the viewport
A 'Re-layout' button recalculates the automatic graph layout (dagre algorithm)
An 'Add Transition' floating action button (absolute positioned, top-left z-10) opens the transition editor panel for creating new transitions
A stats panel shows the count of states, transitions, and the initial (START) state name

Clicking a state node in the graph selects it (highlights the node), deselects any selected transition, closes the new transition form, and opens the State Detail side panel on the right (w-80 width). Clicking the graph background or another node deselects the current selection.
Clicking a transition edge in the graph selects it, deselects any selected state, and opens the Transition Editor side panel on the right. The edge is visually highlighted when selected.

State nodes display element tiles in a grid, color-coded by element type: testid=blue, role=green, text=amber, ui=purple, url/nav=cyan. Each tile shows a type icon and truncated label. Tiles are draggable between state nodes to create transitions or reassign elements.
The initial state node displays a gold 'START' badge to identify it as the entry point of the state machine. Only one state should have the START badge.

The number of state nodes rendered in the ReactFlow graph must equal the number of states in the configuration. Each node must display the state's name. Missing nodes indicate a rendering bug; extra nodes indicate stale data.

The number of transition edges rendered in the graph must equal the number of transitions in the configuration. Each edge connects the from-state(s) to the activate-state(s). Missing edges indicate a rendering bug in the graph component.
The state detail panel has an editable name input field showing the selected state's name
A 'Save Changes' button persists state name/description updates via PATCH /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/ui-bridge-configs/{configId}/states/{stateId}

When a configuration is selected, the State View tab displays all states from the configuration. Each state shows its name and element information. The number of states shown must match the configuration's state count.

Clicking a state in the State View selects it (highlights it). The selectedStateId is shared with the Graph Editor — switching to Graph Editor after selecting a state in State View should show that state selected.
When a configuration has no states (shouldn't normally happen after discovery), the State View handles the empty case gracefully rather than showing an error.
The transition editor has a name input with placeholder 'e.g., Open Settings'
The transition editor shows 'From States' section with toggle buttons for each state. Selected states are highlighted blue. From-states define which states must be active for this transition to fire.
The transition editor shows 'Activate States' section with toggle buttons for each state. Selected states are highlighted green. Activate-states become active when the transition fires.
The transition editor shows 'Exit States' section with toggle buttons for each state. Selected states are highlighted red. Exit-states become inactive when the transition fires.
An 'Add' button allows appending new actions to the transition's action sequence
A 'Create' button submits the new transition via the create transition API. After creation, the transition editor closes and the graph refreshes showing the new edge.

The action type dropdown includes all core SDK action types: click, doubleClick, rightClick, type, clear, select, focus, blur, hover, scroll, check, uncheck, toggle, setValue, drag, submit, reset — plus workflow actions: wait, navigate. These correspond to the actions the qontinui library's UIBridgeRuntime can execute.

When action type is 'click', the editor shows an 'Element ID' input field for specifying which UI Bridge element to click. The element ID uses the format 'type:identifier' (e.g., 'testid:nav-settings', 'role:button-save').
When action type is 'type', the editor shows: text input ('Text to type'), optional clear_first checkbox, and type_delay fields for typing text into UI elements
When action type is 'navigate', the editor shows a URL input field for page navigation
When action type is 'wait', the editor shows a delay input in milliseconds for timed waits between actions
A search input with placeholder 'Search transitions' filters transitions by name
The transitions list header shows 'Transitions' with a count of total transitions

value.textContent: Select a transition to view its details

metadata.description: When no transition is selected, a message says 'Select a transition to view its details'

When a transition with actions is selected, action playback controls are shown with play/pause, step forward/back, and speed selector (0.5x to 4x). The 'Action Playback' heading identifies this section.
A 'From States' section shows toggle buttons for selecting currently active states (blue highlight when selected)
A 'Target States' section shows toggle buttons for selecting destination states (green highlight when selected)
A 'Find Path' button triggers the pathfinding algorithm via backend API
When a path is found, a green 'Path found' message is shown with total cost and step-by-step transition sequence. Each step shows the transition name and the states it moves between.
When no path exists between selected states, a red error message is shown: 'No path found between the selected states.'

When a path is found, the steps are passed to the Graph Editor as highlightedPath. Switching to the Graph Editor tab should visually highlight the path's transition edges, making it easy to trace the route between states.
Export heading 'Export' is shown with a FileJson icon in brand-primary color, followed by a description about UIBridgeRuntime.from_dict() compatibility
A 'Download JSON' button with Download icon exports the state machine as a JSON file. Disabled when no config is selected or while exporting (shows spinner).
A 'Push to Runner' outline button with Upload icon sends the state machine directly to the qontinui-runner at localhost:9876/state-machine/load. Disabled while exporting or pushing.

The export card shows three stats: config name (bold), '{stateCount} states', and '{transitionCount} transitions' in text-xs text-text-muted. The card has bg-surface-secondary background with border-border-primary.
The export panel shows a code reference to 'UIBridgeRuntime.from_dict()' explaining the export format compatibility, rendered in a code element with bg-surface-primary styling

States contain element_ids arrays that reference UI Bridge element identifiers prefixed by type: testid:, role:, text:, ui:, url:, nav:. In the Graph Editor, each element type has a distinct color (testid=blue, role=green, text=amber, ui=purple, url/nav=cyan). Elements that consistently co-occur across renders are grouped into the same state by the discovery algorithm.

State discovery uses the qontinui core library's FingerprintStrategy with element ID fallback. The algorithm: (1) extracts element IDs from render logs by priority — UI Bridge registry ID, data-testid, HTML id; (2) builds a presence matrix mapping each render to the elements it contains; (3) computes co-occurrence counts showing how often each pair of elements appears together; (4) groups elements with high co-occurrence into states. Strategy 'auto' selects between fingerprint-based (when pre-computed fingerprint data from the SDK is available) and element-ID fallback (synthesizes fingerprints from element IDs). Elements are classified by position zone (header, footer, sidebar, main, modal) which affects scope: header/footer elements may be marked global (present in many states), modal elements indicate blocking states. Results include states with confidence scores (0.0-1.0) based on co-occurrence strength, unique_element_count, render_count, and strategy_used.
Discovery panel cards use p-4 outer padding with max-w-4xl centered layout. Inner card content uses py-3 padding with space-y-3 vertical spacing between elements.

The connected app banner uses a green theme: bg-green-500/10 background with border-green-500/20 border and rounded-md. The CheckCircle2 icon and text should be clearly visible against this semi-transparent green background.

The export panel uses max-w-lg centered layout with p-6 spacing. The export info card uses bg-surface-secondary background with border-border-primary border and rounded-lg. The UIBridgeRuntime.from_dict() code reference uses bg-surface-primary with px-1 py-0.5 rounded styling.

Dragging an element tile from one state node to another state node in the Graph Editor creates a new transition between the two states (if no transition exists) or reassigns the element to the target state (updating element_ids). The drop target state is visually highlighted during drag-over. When the element is dropped, the appropriate API call is made and the graph refreshes.

During a drag operation, the isDragging state is true and the dropTargetStateId identifies which state is being hovered over. The drop target state node should show visual feedback (highlight or border change) to indicate it will accept the drop.
A search input in the state list sidebar filters states by name, state ID, or element ID. Typing a query should filter the visible state list to only matching states.

Two view mode buttons (List and Spatial) toggle between a scrollable state list and a force-directed spatial canvas showing states as circles with transitions as arrows and shared-element dashed connections.
Each state's confidence percentage is color-coded: green for ≥80%, amber for ≥50%, and red for <50%. This indicates the algorithm's certainty in grouping elements into that state.

When a state is selected in the detail panel, its elements are grouped by type (e.g., role:button, text:input, url:link) with type-colored badges. Each badge shows the element ID. A maximum of 20 elements are shown, with a '+N more' truncation if exceeded.

Each state in the list shows the count of outgoing and incoming transitions as small arrow-based indicators. This allows quick identification of dead-end states (no outgoing) or unreachable states (no incoming).

When a state is selected, any domain knowledge entries are shown as cards with title (bold), content (line-clamped to 2 lines), and tags as small badges. If no domain knowledge exists, this section is absent.

The playback system animates each action type differently: click shows a blue ripple effect, type shows character-by-character text reveal with a blinking cursor, navigate shows a bouncing arrow, select shows a bouncing chevron, and wait shows a spinning ring. Actions play sequentially based on computed durations.
A speed control dropdown offers 0.5x, 1x, 1.5x, 2x, and 4x playback speeds. Changing speed affects the duration of subsequent action animations.

An action timeline shows numbered dots for each action, color-coded by action type. Dots show state: green checkmark for completed (past), animated icon for current, and gray for future. Clicking a dot jumps playback to that action.

Playback controls include: Reset (back to start), Step Back (previous action), Play/Pause toggle, and Step Forward (next action). A progress bar shows overall completion, and text shows 'Action N of M' during playback or 'Ready to play' when idle.

Two dropdown filters ('From states' and 'To states') in the transitions sidebar filter the transition list to show only transitions matching the selected from/to states. Combined with the search input, this provides multi-dimensional filtering.

Acceptance criteria are displayed as a bulleted list. Clicking a criterion makes it editable inline — pressing Enter or blurring saves the edit, pressing Escape cancels. Submitting an empty value deletes the criterion. New criteria can be added via an input with placeholder 'Add acceptance criterion...'.

Domain knowledge entries are shown as cards with title, content (line-clamped to 2 lines), and tag badges. An '+ Add' button reveals a form with title input (autofocus), content textarea, and comma-separated tags input. Each card has a hover-visible remove button. Domain knowledge describes contextual information about a state that helps AI understand its purpose.

The elements section shows a scrollable list (max 192px height) of element badges colored by type. An input with placeholder 'type:element-id' and Add button allows adding new elements. Hovering over an element badge reveals a remove button to delete it from the state.

A 2×2 grid displays state metadata: State ID (monospace, break-all), confidence percentage (with dynamic color matching the confidence color scheme: green ≥80%, amber ≥50%, red <50%), element count, and render count.

Pressing '?' or clicking the help button in the graph toolbar toggles a keyboard shortcuts overlay (bottom-right) listing all available shortcuts: Esc (deselect), ? (toggle help), F (fit view), L (re-layout), +/- (zoom), Tab/Shift+Tab (cycle states), I (initial state), Delete (delete transition). Additional entries show 'Create transition: Drag element' and 'Move element: Alt+Drag'.

In the Graph Editor: pressing F fits the graph to the viewport; pressing L re-runs the dagre layout algorithm; pressing Tab cycles forward through state nodes; pressing Shift+Tab cycles backward; pressing I selects and centers the initial state.

When a transition edge is selected in the Graph Editor, pressing Delete or Backspace calls the delete transition API and removes it from the graph. The transition must be selected first (click on the edge).

The Graph Editor displays a MiniMap in the corner showing an overview of the entire graph. State nodes in the minimap are colored: gold for the initial state, amber for blocking states, and the brand-primary color for regular states.

Discovery identifies elements by type priority: (1) ui-id from the UI Bridge element registry, (2) data-testid attributes, (3) HTML id attributes (when include_html_ids is true), (4) fingerprint-based identification. The discovery results preview and state element lists should show element IDs that follow the format 'type:identifier' (e.g., 'role:button:Submit', 'text:input:search', 'url:/settings').

States are formed by element co-occurrence: elements that consistently appear together across multiple render logs are grouped into the same state. If element A and element B always appear in the same renders, they belong to the same state. If element C appears only in renders where A and B are absent, it belongs to a different state. The state preview list in the discovery results should reflect this grouping — each state's element set should be internally consistent (elements that co-occur) and externally distinct (different from other states' element sets).

If discovery is run with only a single render log, exactly one state should be created containing all elements from that render, with 100% confidence. No transitions should exist since there's no navigation between states to detect. The discovery results should show '1 states' badge and '0 renders' is incorrect — it should show '1 renders'.
Contexts heading is present at the top of the page
Sidebar list area exists for displaying context documents
New context button is present for creating new context documents
Search input field is initially empty when the page loads
Users can create, edit, delete, and duplicate context documents from the sidebar list
Users can write markdown content for each context using a Monaco code editor
Users can configure auto-include rules with keywords, action types, and error patterns so contexts attach automatically to matching workflows
The page heading 'Step Builders' is rendered at the top of the page
A grid layout of builder cards is present for navigating to step editors
Builder cards are rendered as clickable links (enabled), allowing navigation to each step editor
Each builder card contains descriptive text explaining the step type it covers
Users can see all available step types with descriptions to choose the right builder for their needs
Page heading 'Workflows' is present
Sidebar workflow list area is present
Main content area is present
Search input for filtering workflows is present
New Workflow button is present in sidebar
AI generation heading is visible
AI generation heading is visible
Prompt textarea for describing the desired workflow is present
Generate button is disabled when prompt textarea is empty
Run button is present when workflow is loaded
Run button is present when workflow is loaded
Save button is present when workflow is loaded
Export button is present when workflow is loaded
Setup phase section is present
Verification phase section is present
Agentic phase section is present
Completion phase section is present
Users can create, search, duplicate, and delete workflows from the sidebar library
Users can organize workflow steps into four execution phases: setup, verification, agentic, and completion
Users can switch to AI generation mode to have workflows created automatically from a natural-language prompt
Main content area has appropriate ARIA landmark role
Sidebar has complementary ARIA landmark role
Search input has searchbox ARIA role for assistive technology

Users can create a new chat session to start a conversation with Claude. The 'New Chat' button on the landing page initiates a WebSocket session with the connected runner and navigates to the active session view.

Users can type and send messages to Claude in a real-time conversation. Messages are sent by pressing Enter (Shift+Enter for newlines) or clicking the send button. The AI responds with markdown-formatted messages that stream in real-time.

Users can type and send messages to Claude in a real-time conversation. Messages are sent by pressing Enter (Shift+Enter for newlines) or clicking the send button. The AI responds with markdown-formatted messages that stream in real-time.

Users can view the full conversation history in a scrollable message area. User messages appear right-aligned with brand-colored backgrounds; AI messages appear left-aligned with markdown rendering (code blocks, lists, links, etc.).

Users can interrupt the AI while it is processing/responding. When the session is in 'processing' state, the send button is replaced by an interrupt (stop) button that cancels the current AI response.

Users can generate an executable multi-phase workflow from the conversation. After at least 2 messages have been exchanged, a 'Generate Workflow' button appears. Clicking it sends the conversation context to the AI, which produces a structured workflow with setup, verification, agentic, and completion phases.

Users can preview the generated workflow in a side panel that shows the workflow name, description, tags, and all steps organized by execution phase (setup, verification, agentic, completion). The layout splits into a 50/50 chat-on-left, workflow-on-right view.
Users can execute the generated workflow directly from the preview panel without leaving the chat page
Users can open the generated workflow in the Workflow Builder (/build/workflows) for detailed editing of individual steps, phases, and configuration
Users can save the generated workflow to the workflow library for later use, making it available from the Workflow Builder sidebar
Users can regenerate the workflow if the initial result is unsatisfactory. This clears the current workflow and re-runs generation with the same conversation context.
Users can rename a chat session by clicking the session name in the header, which switches to an inline text input. The name auto-generates from the first message but can be customized at any time.
Users can close the current chat session and return to the landing page. The close button is in the session header.
Users can see the current session state (Ready, Processing, Disconnected, Closed) via a color-coded badge in the header and a label above the input area, so they know when the AI is available or busy

Users can see whether the runner is connected or offline. A runner connection is required to create sessions and send messages. The status appears on both the landing page header and the session header.
Users can toggle whether the generated workflow should include UI Bridge SDK integration instructions. This checkbox is persisted to localStorage and appears alongside the Generate Workflow button.
Chat landing page root container is present
Page heading 'Chat' is present
Runner connection status indicator is present in the landing page header
Session list area is present (shows previous sessions or empty state)
Chat session page root container is present
Chat panel containing messages and input is present
Workflow preview panel container appears when a workflow is generated (right half of split layout)
Chat header bar is present
Clickable session name for inline editing is present
Runner connection status indicator in session header is present
Chat input container is present
Message textarea with auto-resize (min 42px, max 120px) is present
Send button is present when session is not processing
Interrupt (stop) button replaces the send button while AI is processing
Session state label (Ready/Processing/Connecting/Disconnected/Session Closed) is displayed above the input
UI Bridge toggle checkbox appears alongside the Generate Workflow button
Scrollable messages container is present
Streaming indicator (animated dots or partial markdown) appears while AI is generating a response
Scroll-to-bottom button appears when user has scrolled up from the latest messages
Close button to dismiss the workflow preview panel
Collapsible phase sections container showing workflow steps grouped by phase
Regenerate button to re-generate the workflow from conversation
Loading spinner appears while workflow is being generated
Workflow loading state displays a spinner with 'Generating workflow...' text
Successful generation displays the phases container with collapsible step sections
Execute button is visible when workflow generation succeeds
Empty state with prompt text is shown when no messages have been sent yet
The Execute page heading is rendered at the top of the page
The workflow library panel is present for browsing available workflows
The sequence builder panel is present for assembling the execution queue
The Run button is disabled when the execution sequence is empty, preventing accidental runs with no workflows queued
The Run button is disabled when the execution sequence is empty, preventing accidental runs with no workflows queued
The Save button is present so users can persist their assembled sequences for future reuse
The Runners page heading is rendered at the top of the page
The 'Active Connections' tab button exists for viewing currently connected runners
The 'Connection History' tab button exists for viewing past runner connections
The Active Runs page heading is rendered at the top of the page
The tab bar for switching between Dashboard and widget views is present
Timeline tab is present for viewing step execution progress
AI Conversation tab is present for viewing live AI output
Commands tab is present for viewing command execution details
UI Bridge tab is present for viewing UI interaction logs
Verification tab is present for viewing verification results
Findings tab is present for viewing detected issues
Flow tab is present for viewing flow execution details
Status tab is present for viewing current run status
An empty state message is shown when there are no active workflow runs
An empty state message is shown when there are no active workflow runs
A primary widget is displayed in the main content area showing detailed execution data
The active widget displays a heading with the widget name (using data-content-label='widget-heading')
Summary cards display widget names using data-content-label='widget-name' for identification
Step progress is displayed showing completed steps out of total (e.g. '3/10 steps')
When a run has not started executing steps, a waiting message is shown
AI session count is displayed (e.g. '1 session' or '2 sessions')
AI message count is displayed (e.g. '5 msgs')
When no AI messages exist, an empty state is shown
Verification pass count is displayed (e.g. '4/5 passed')
When no verification results exist, a placeholder message is shown
Findings count metric is displayed with data-content-label='findings-count'
When no findings exist, 'No issues found' status is displayed
Command count is displayed (e.g. '3 commands')
When no commands have been executed, a placeholder message is shown
UI Bridge action count is displayed (e.g. '5 actions')
When no UI Bridge actions have been executed, a placeholder is shown
The current execution phase is visible as a badge on the run card or status widget
The ActiveRunsBar is visible when multiple runs are active, showing selectable run cards
The ActiveRunsBar is hidden when only a single run is active to avoid unnecessary UI clutter
The page provides live real-time updates as workflows progress through execution phases, with widgets reflecting current state without manual refresh
Findings heading is present at the top of the page
Critical severity summary card or badge is present showing critical finding count
High severity summary card or badge is present showing high finding count
Medium severity summary card or badge is present showing medium finding count
Low severity summary card or badge is present showing low finding count
Severity filter control is enabled and interactive for user filtering
High severity filter control is enabled and interactive
